data.title: I'm Bullish On RF Industries With Eyes Wide Open
data.description:
Thesis

RF Industries, Ltd. (RFIL) is a microcap out of San Diego. They design and make interconnect products. Think telecom, data networking, defense, and aerospace. Now, the interesting part.

For the past year, the stock has been on a real breakout. It’s more than doubled over the past year. Meanwhile, the S&P 500 (SP500) hasn’t come close to that kind of move.

After reviewing the latest earnings, I’m leaning bullish. The company is clearly opening up steadier growth across a mix of end markets, which, at least to me, looks pretty durable.

RF Industries' Shift To Higher-Quality Growth

  • Net sales grew 23% year-over-year to $22.7 million.
  • Gross profit margin of 37% exceeded our 30% target and adjusted EBITDA was 11.5% of net sales, above our stated goal of 10%.
  • For the full fiscal year, net sales were $80.6 million, an increase of 24% compared to fiscal 2024.
  • Gross profit margin for the year was 33% compared to 29% in the prior year.
  • We delivered adjusted EBITDA of $6.1 million, a huge increase compared to $838,000 in adjusted EBITDA in fiscal 2024.

  • We supported more than 130 projects across these categories delivering a meaningful contribution to revenue compared to prior years.

  • Fourth quarter adjusted EBITDA was $2.6 million, compared to adjusted EBITDA of $908,000 for Q4 2024.

To begin with, the principal driver appears to be diversification. The company’s selling fewer loose parts and more bundled systems, packages that just happen to cost more. As a result, and not coincidentally, revenues have expanded even as margins have improved. This shift, moreover, tends to deepen customer relationships, since it draws RF into larger, more complex projects and, in so doing, makes pricing more durable.

Management, for its part, is characterizing this as a breakout (noted a couple of times in the prepared remarks), and it's not difficult to see why. Demand, after all, materialized across several end markets more or less simultaneously. Infrastructure projects, like stadiums and transportation, were supportive, insofar as these customers are making long-term commitments to connectivity. Aerospace and defense, meanwhile, gave an element of stability, largely because RF worked closely with customers on compliant designs. Meanwhile, telecom also played a role, especially as carriers packed networks more tightly to boost coverage and reliability.

That said, I think it'd be unwise to accept the optimism entirely at face value. The risks in question relate less to structure than to timing. Remember, the industry's still dealing with projects that start and stop unexpectedly, along with remaining supply-chain problems. These issues can slow down when revenue comes in, even though customer demand itself hasn't changed.

  • Our inventory was $13.7 million down from $14.7 million last year.
  • Our improved results allowed us to reduce our net debt by $4.6 million compared to last year.
  • As of October 31, 2025, we had a total of $5.1 million of cash and cash equivalents.
  • We had working capital of $14.1 million and a current ratio of approximately 1.7:1, with current assets of $35 million and current liabilities of $20.9 million.
  • As of October 31, 2025, we had borrowed $7.8 million from our revolving credit facility.

Cash flow got better because they tightened inventory and cut costs. On the cost side, they kept expenses in check by fixing processes and upgrading IT. That helped productivity. At the same time, management seems clear-eyed about reality that wages and benefits for U.S. production workers are still going up. It's a structural pressure, but it’s being offset for now by efficiency gains.

You can see the same mindset on the balance sheet. Net debt came down, which tells us working capital is healthier. Part of that came from renegotiating credit terms, which lowered borrowing costs.

As for revenue, telecom now makes up roughly half the business, which means it’s no longer the all-consuming engine it used to be; instead, aerospace, industrial, and public-safety activities are filling in.

Competition, of course, hasn't gone away. To protect recurring revenue, channel partners and OEMs still expect steady innovation. I wouldn't see this as a balance-sheet problem so much as an issue of timing and product cycles that management has to keep addressing.

RF Industries' Strategic Reset

Some of you may remember when the company had to unload assets that never really fit, like the Comnet sale in 2018. RF sold it only 3.5 years later for around the same amount as the original purchase price. Mistakes happen. At least it stripped away distractions, narrowed the focus to interconnects, and made the model simple, which mostly explains how it ended up where it is today.

At the same time, the macro backdrop's definitely helped. Telecom operators are under pressure to spend less but get more. As AI workloads took off, they didn’t try to build everything in-house. They narrowed the vendor list and aimed capital spending at infrastructure that could handle a lot more data, a dynamic that directly supports this company’s positioning. And we've all seen how defense spending has increased, driven by government focus on satellite systems and security, which has helped boost aerospace performance.

But favorable conditions don’t automatically translate into better economics. Margins picked up for two reasons. First, sales were higher. Second, the mix improved. As a result, the fixed costs, especially labor costs, were finally fully paid for. Once that happened, a lot more cash started dropping straight to the bottom line. And that’s hard to pull off. Inventory moves were about matching supply to demand under a supply chain warped by tariffs and uncertainty. The whole operation braced for sudden hits from logistics costs or other product expenses that could spike without warning.

From a day-to-day operations' perspective, the fourth quarter showed ongoing improvement in how consistently the company runs its business and how well it controls inventory levels, costs, and delivery timing. Updates to processes and information technology systems are improving the accuracy of forecasts and increasing visibility and the ability to scale operations across the entire organization. According to management, customers are now looping them in much earlier in the design process. In other words, they’re no longer just a parts' vendor. They’re starting to look more like a problem-solving partner. Which is why they naturally uncovered more chances to cross-sell and deepen the relationship.

RFIL's Valuation

RFIL trades at about 23x earnings, you can see from the graph above that that's well above where fair value sits, closer to 15x on normalized earnings. So, in other words, the stock's already carries a big premium. Even so, investors are clearly expecting the company to grow its profits faster. This expectation is mainly tied to the company’s move toward offering more complete, solution-based products and services.

Based on consensus estimates, the forward non-GAAP P/E ratio falls to 21.77x in 2026 and 19.91x in 2027. These estimates already assume EPS growth of 7.5% to 9.3%. This path appears achievable to me if the company keeps doing what they're doing. This is a cyclical business, so project timing can still swing results a lot from one quarter to the next. If, for example, deals in the pipeline slip and don’t turn into shipments on time, then the operating leverage that’s boosting results right now would fade pretty quickly.

Right now, there’s no major cash burn and only minor dilution. In fact, paying down debt has cleaned up the balance sheet. Nevertheless, at this valuation, and since micro-caps are usually more volatile, the margin for error is pretty thin.

Final Takeaway

All things considered, I’m going to rate this as a highly speculative buy. I think the "breakout" will continue and there's room for more positive momentum from here. On the flip side, the downside hits faster if any projects slip and the P/E slides back toward the fair value ratio, which is very possible, especially since this is a microcap. I’d say risk-tolerant small-cap investors who are patient and willing to scale in should also buy it. Conservative investors, or anyone with a longer time horizon, this isn't for you.

data.title: I Wouldn't Want To Retire Without These Income Machines
data.description:
Sequence of returns risk is probably the most underrated risk that faces retirees. This is because if you go through a prolonged period of material underperformance in your portfolio during retirement and have to liquidate shares at a deep discount to their intrinsic value, you can materially shorten your portfolio's ability, or the number of years over which your portfolio can fund your living expenses, even if the portfolio goes on to outperform at other times. Put differently, it would be like a six-foot-tall person drowning while trying to cross a river that was only four feet deep on average because there was a 20-foot-long span where the river was 10 feet deep, and he was unable to swim.

On top of that, investors are today looking at a market where equities are priced at very rich levels, and even Howard Marks is warning that we could be in for a lost decade for the S&P 500 (SPY) ahead. This means that investors who are depending on the stock market to deliver steady and attractive returns for the foreseeable future are taking on enormous risk. As a result, I believe that investing instead in durable, dependable, and stagflation-resistant income machines is indispensable to a retiree's portfolio today because such securities help to mitigate sequence of returns risk as well as economic risk at the same time. With that in view, in this article, I'm going to detail two income machines that I would not want to retire without, especially in the current environment.

Indispensable Retirement Income Machine #1

The first income machine is high-quality midstream energy infrastructure (MLPX). The reason I like this sector so much right now is that its yields are quite high, with most offerings in the range of 5% to 9% right now. Additionally, these payouts are well covered by cash flows that are backed by fee-based, contracted, and/or regulated assets. Moreover, these contracts and regulated assets tend to have inflation protection attached to them, which, when combined with the bond-like and/or toll road-type cash flow streams that they generate, make them remarkable stagflation-resistant investment opportunities. On top of that, when you factor in their tax advantages, especially from MLPs in which their distributions tend to be classified as a return of capital, and the fact that they're able to consistently grow their payouts in the current environment, they are very powerful income machines for a retiree looking to minimize sequence of returns risk.

My confidence in their ability to sustain their distributions through all sorts of economic environments is further strengthened by the fact that they have very strong balance sheets right now, with very little debt relative to their past histories, well-laddered maturities, significant liquidity, and free cash flow generation, as well as the ability to self-fund their growth projects in most cases. Additionally, they have strong investment-grade credit ratings in most cases. For example, Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) has an A- credit rating, and Enbridge (ENB) has a BBB+ credit rating. Given that these are two of the very highest credit ratings in the space, these companies are also some of my favorite picks in the sector for retirees looking for stable income.

EPD, for example, has 27 consecutive years of distribution growth, a sector-best A- credit rating, and inflation-protected fee-based cash flows with relatively low commodity price exposure. They also have a massive NGL export footprint that sets them up to benefit from an NGL export boom for years to come. They also have a strong organic growth backlog that is coming online primarily this year that will deliver a step-size increase in free cash flow while reducing their leverage ratio substantially. This should make their 7% distribution yield and their ability to grow it at a mid-single-digit CAGR very sustainable for years to come.

ENB, meanwhile, has a highly diversified cash flow stream with about 98% of its cash flow either regulated or tied to take-or-pay contracts, making it very bond-like in nature. Additionally, 80% of its EBITDA is inflation-protected, and it has strong LNG and gas transmission growth runways. Not only that, but its counterparties are over 95% investment grade, further cementing its status as a very stable dividend payer. When combined with its over quarter-century of consecutive dividend growth and very attractive dividend yield, ENB is a very attractive candidate for inclusion in an income-focused retiree's portfolio.

For investors who want even further diversification, the Alerian MLP ETF (AMLP) is a good option, as it issues a 1099 while giving exposure to K-1 issuing MLPs like EPD, while also paying out an 8.2% distribution yield and growing payouts at a very strong rate. In fact, I recently rated AMLP as one of only three high-yield, high-growth, high-dividend-yield, and high-dividend-growth ETFs in the market today.

Indispensable Retirement Income Machine #2

The second income machine I would not want to retire without is gold bullion leases. While gold (GLD) is traditionally viewed as a non-income-producing asset, bullion leases are changing that, as they enable investors to fully own physical gold bullion but earn a competitive yield on that bullion that is close to what you can earn from many REITs (VNQ) like Essential Properties Realty Trust (EPRT) or Mid-America Apartment Communities (MAA), all of which yield around 4% at the moment.

Not only that, but the yield is paid in gold ounces, so as gold appreciates over the long term, so will the yield. Additionally, by investing in these leases, you do not have to pay storage fees or an expense ratio like you would investing in a gold ETF or owning gold in a vault. While not risk-free, the leases I invest in are fully insured and are not on the counterparty's balance sheet, thereby providing a fairly low-risk way to generate an attractive yield on top of uncapped upside on the gold bullion.

In addition to the attractive structure of this income machine, the reason I am so bullish on gold right now is because the U.S. government continues to run massive deficits, which, when combined with the large existing debt, raise concerns about the long-term solvency of the country and the value of the dollar, as the government may have to pivot toward further debasement and inflation to deal with its debt, which would be very bullish for gold.

Additionally, there's accelerating de-dollarization across the world right now, with central banks leading the charge by aggressively replacing dollar-denominated reserves with gold, thereby providing a very strong, consistent, and, in many cases, price-insensitive bid on gold, which has contributed tremendously to its strong performance in recent years. However, this trend has shown no signs of slowing down, which bodes very well for gold moving forward.

Last but not least, the end of the peace dividend and the potential imminence of a China threat have been heralded by the United Kingdom and the United States government officials, respectively, in recent years. This also bodes very well for gold because, in the event that a major conflict were to break out between major powers, especially involving the world's two largest economies in the United States and China, particularly over such an economically valuable island as Taiwan, it would likely send global markets tumbling lower while gold would likely soar as a safe haven of choice in such a scenario.

Even if the U.S. government can somehow get its fiscal house in order and/or war with China is avoided, I think gold will likely still do okay over time because of the ongoing de-dollarization trend. Additionally, the potentially catastrophic impacts of one or more of these risks playing out in the not-too-distant future on retirees are significant enough that it is worth owning gold as a form of portfolio and income stream insurance.

Investor Takeaway

With midstream having inflation-linked escalators on its cash flows and gold intrinsically being an inflation hedge over time, especially as a guard against long-term debasement and/or macroeconomic and geopolitical turmoil, these two positions combine nicely to not only deliver an attractive yield that should hold up in an economic downturn, but also one that should be quite resilient in the face of inflation and other macro factors that would otherwise be very challenging to a portfolio that's completely focused on traditional dividend growth stocks, such as those seen in the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD). As a result, midstream infrastructure and gold bullion leases are my two largest allocations in my low beta, 7% to 8% yielding, and total return outperforming portfolios at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Construction Outlook 2026: Growth Returns To The European Construction Sector
data.description:
By Maurice van Sante, Senior Economist Construction & Team Lead Sectors

2026: Growth returns

We forecast that growth will return to the EU construction sector in 2026. Production is set to increase by 1.5%. This comes after a drop of 1.5% in 2024 and zero growth in 2025. More positive signals are popping up. For instance, the confidence indicator for the EU construction sector is clearly improving. While still negative, the December reading of -3.3 was the highest in more than two years.

The building sector has declined, while infrastructure has grown

It’s mainly the building sector (consisting of residential and non-residential activities) that has suffered in previous years. The main causes are higher interest rates, increased building costs, and consumers and companies thinking twice before investing in new premises amid uncertain economic circumstances. On the other hand, the specialised construction sector has profited from investments in renovation and sustainability works. The infrastructure sector has also provided some counterbalance through EU funds and investments in energy works and digital infrastructure.

Developments in subsectors differ

In France, construction volume declined by 1.5% in 2025. The confidence indicator for the construction sector in France remains low, and it’s not improving. The issuance of housing permits improved slightly at the beginning of 2025 but has shown signs of decline since August. Given low GDP growth, we forecast only 0.5% growth for the French construction sector in 2026.

EU construction forecast

For the Netherlands, we also expect low growth rates. After a significant 2.9% contraction in 2024, we expect Dutch building volumes to increase by just 0.5% in both 2025 and 2026. Housing construction is particularly weak. Structural bottlenecks are, like in many countries, a many-headed monster. They include a shortage of building land, lengthy and complex permitting requirements, objection procedures and grid congestion. The Dutch-specific nitrogen issue (new building projects often require permits proving they won’t increase nitrogen emissions) also leads to additional project postponements and cancellations.

After five years of decline, the German construction industry is projected to return to growth in 2026. As the largest construction sector in Europe, it contracted by more than 10% between 2020 and 2025. For 2026, a 2.5% growth rate is anticipated, driven by a gradual recovery in the new residential market and increased infrastructure investment.

Spain is also showing positive developments. The Spanish construction sector faced a significant decline between 2019 and 2022, losing more than 25% of its volume. However, the Spanish market has been recovering since 2023. We forecast 2.5% growth in 2026. In recent years, the number of building permits issued has surged. Additionally, contractor confidence in Spain is strong, and the construction industry is benefiting from robust GDP growth.

Residential building

Recovery in new residential building

There has been some improvement in the new residential market. The number of permits issued for new houses is an important indicator. Typically, it takes between one and two years from the granting of a permit to the completion of a new home. The number of permits has been increasing since the beginning of 2025. By September 2025, the level was 26% higher than a year earlier.

Housing shortages remain severe in many urban areas across Europe. Additionally, wages have increased, building material costs have largely stopped rising and housing prices are on the rise again despite slightly increased interest rates in November and December 2025. This makes the business case for new housing projects more attractive. Permit issuance is still low compared to 2021, but we expect some improvement in the residential sector in 2026 and 2027.

Recovery in number of issued building permits

Germany follows the general European trend of improvement. Yet German building permits for new houses declined even more sharply in 2021 and 2022. To speed up the building process, the German government has announced the ‘housing construction turbo (Bau-Turbo)’ to expedite planning and authorisation of new projects in popular living areas. In France, there is also a gradual improvement. However, the latest data for September and October show that the upswing remains fragile.

The number of building permits for new houses issued in the Netherlands in 2025 is only slightly higher than in 2024. The disappointing growth is due to various chronic bottlenecks, including a shortage of (affordable) building land, complex project development processes and legal delays. Housing projects are regularly hindered by overloads on the electricity grid, which postpones new construction amid limited connection possibilities. In addition, the nitrogen issue, particularly relevant for the Netherlands, is causing further delays to construction projects because, in some cases, permits cannot be granted due to nitrogen emissions.

Belgium is one of the few countries that still faces a decline in the number of building permits issued. Belgium, like many other countries, struggles with time-consuming project procedures. However, that is not the whole narrative. A reduced VAT rate of 6% now applies to the demolition and reconstruction of homes. For those weighing the option of a completely new build (subject to 21% VAT) versus demolishing an existing home and rebuilding, the VAT difference may be a decisive factor.

The European affordable housing plan

Published by the European Commission in December 2025, the European Affordable Housing Plan sets out a coordinated response to the continent’s housing shortages. Recognising housing as a basic right and key to social stability, the plan proposes four pillars of action: increasing supply, mobilising investment, simplifying regulations and supporting vulnerable groups. It aims to add 650,000 extra homes annually across the EU (on top of the current 1.6 million built per year), while streamlining construction rules and promoting sustainable, energy-efficient housing.

Together with this plan, the European Strategy for Housing Construction was presented. It aims to make Europe’s construction industry more competitive, productive, and innovative to address the continent’s housing affordability crisis. The strategy focuses on simplifying and digitalising permitting procedures, boosting innovation and the use of sustainable materials, and improving access to skilled labour. Key measures include harmonising standards, supporting modular and off-site construction and leveraging financial instruments to accelerate the deployment of new technologies.

The European Investment Bank has announced €400m in financing for new technologies with the potential to increase productivity and reduce the construction and development costs of new housing.

Non-residential building

The non-residential building sector occupies approximately 30% of total construction output in the European Union. It is a very diverse subsector consisting of the construction of healthcare and education buildings, as well as industrial and office buildings. The latter are the largest sub-markets with a share of approximately 18% and 16% in 2025, respectively.

Office and industrial market under pressure

Some sub-markets have faced headwinds in recent years. The office market, in particular, has been negatively affected by the hybrid work trend and economic uncertainties. This has resulted in a sharp decline in the EU permit issuing for new offices. During the third quarter of 2025, the amount of floor space for new offices declined by more than 30% compared to 2022.

In addition, new orders from the manufacturing sector are falling short due to low production levels and low capacity utilisation across many sectors amid fierce international competition and high energy prices in Europe. Yet, new investments in defence and the money that must be spent in 2026 before the European Recovery and Resilience Facility ends (most relevant for southern European countries) are promising a return to growth for European manufacturing.

Health market and renovation show resistance

Some positive counterforces in the non-residential sector are coming from public investments in healthcare and educational buildings. In addition, renovation in the non-residential market is encouraged by energy-efficiency requirements and sustainability incentives. Therefore, despite a decline in the office market, we expect growth in most residential sub-markets in 2026 and 2027.

Sharp decline of issued permits for offices

Infrastructure sector

Growth in infrastructure sector

The infrastructure sector demonstrates greater resilience to economic fluctuations compared to the non-residential sector. This is primarily due to its reliance on investments from local and national governments. In 2025, EU infrastructure investments increased by approximately 1%, thereby contributing to overall construction production volumes.

The subsector continues to benefit from essential investments in the energy transition, notably in new power grids and digital infrastructure. Conversely, emerging challenges are evident in drinking water, as both population growth and climate change lead to more frequent droughts across many regions.

Highest infrastructure sector growth in the Netherlands

The Dutch infrastructure sector has witnessed the strongest growth rates in recent years. In 2025, volumes are likely to have increased by around 7%. We expect lower growth in 2026. The volume increases are mainly due to increased maintenance and investment to comply with the European Water Framework Directive and to support climate adaptation. This leads to projects such as sewerage improvement and flood defence reinforcement.

The nitrogen issue (new building projects often require permits proving they won’t increase nitrogen emissions) is still a burden, especially for new projects. In response, the central government put some large new infrastructure projects on hold and transferred these funds to renovation and maintenance work that often doesn’t require a specific permit. In addition, billions of euros are invested in expanding the Dutch power grid and advancing the energy transition.

In Germany, infrastructure investment rose by about 1.5% in 2025. Germany last year introduced a €500bn plan for infrastructure and climate investments, aiming to expand further in the years ahead. The infrastructure fund, however, is not entirely new. It also involves reallocating money from the regular budget, with a significant amount designated for railway projects. Thanks to bureaucratic hurdles in Germany, these projects are unlikely to reach the market before the end of 2026.

By the end of 2025, an EC survey shows that German infrastructure builders remain pessimistic about their order books. However, another survey (the RICS market survey) indicates that German infrastructure professionals are now significantly more optimistic about their outlook for the next 12 months – especially after the announcement of the infrastructure fund. This optimism aligns with expectations that the first substantial investments will occur by the end of 2026.

Infrastructure companies in Germany, France and Belgium pessimistic about their order books

In Belgium, infrastructure volumes declined slightly in 2025. Furthermore, contractors in the infrastructure sector are expressing greater pessimism about their order books. This sentiment is largely due to two major projects – the Oosterweel (Ringroad Antwerp) and Princess Elisabeth Island (offshore wind energy hub) – having reached their construction peaks. Additionally, investments from local authorities have also plateaued following the 2024 municipal elections.

Building prices

No substantial price increases expected

Few contractors planned to increase their sales prices in December 2025. Contractors do not need to pass on higher input costs, and they are still facing strong competition due to production declines in 2024 and zero growth in 2025. However, the proportion of EU contractors planning to raise sales prices has risen slightly, on balance, from 7% in December 2024 to 9% in December 2025.

More – but still few – contractors plan to raise sales prices

In particular, more contractors in the Netherlands intend to demand higher prices over the next three months. On balance, more than 40% of Dutch contractors are planning to increase their sales prices. Few German builders still intend to raise sales prices, but thanks to improving market conditions, more of them see opportunities to do so. By comparison, in France, the majority of building firms are even planning to decrease sales prices amid sluggish demand.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

data.title: Bayer: Timing Upside In 2025, More Upside Possible But Risky In 2026E (Downgrade)
data.description:
In this article, I'll be providing my typical half-year update for Bayer A.G. (BAYZF) (FWB: BAYN). Last time around, though I was a bit "late" at the time, I rated the company a compelling Buy in 2025 due to valuation and potential reversal upside. It pleases me greatly to be able to say that this valuation upside has come. and is outperforming the SPDR® S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY) by a factor of 5-6x.

I'm therefore sitting on a significant short-term return - over 100% annualized, in fact—and you can find that previous article here.

Was my investment luck, or was it, in fact, some actual fact and consideration involved? It's impossible for anyone, including me, to really forecast the company and say when something is reversing, or going to reverse, or how far. If I could do that, I'd probably be one of the richest alive at this point - but no one can do that. What I can do, or believe that I'm able to do, is target undervalued equity with a good allocation, and I have the inherent patience to wait for that reversal to arrive.

My previous investments should show you this, and Bayer is only the latest line of examples in many such investments that I cover. Some of them don't turn out positive until years and years later - but the reason that I use "valuation" as my primary method for investing is that I don't experience many total investment failures or capital losses. It would essentially require for a company that I designate as "fundamentally safe" to entirely fall apart. It does happen, but it rarely happens, because my requirements for these investments are so high.

Bayer is/was actually one of the higher-risk investments that I had or have focused on for the last few years. Not because of the company size, which is massive, but because of the inherent company litigation risk from M&A risk in the form of glyphosate litigation.

It's my continued conviction that the $63B acquisition of Monsanto was neither positive nor at a good value for Bayer and that the litigation risk and share price performance for the past 8 years would not have been the same without it. By the way, this is not just my opinion - the M&A is often being cited as one of history's worst M&A, destroying over €30B in market capitalization shortly after finalization. I believe the company made a mistake doing this, but I also believe we're starting to see the light at the end of the tunnel. But it shouldn't surprise you that my faith in Bayer management or company management/capital allocation is far from the highest in the companies I have on my coverage spectrum.

It is further my conviction that the company will recover and is on the way to doing so now, and I'm enjoying being a part of that recovery.

Bayer After 2025 - an Excellent Contrarian Idea, as I Forecasted at the Time

My last Bayer article was actually part of a competition at the time -the best contrarian idea, and with a 60%+ RoR over the 10%+ RoR in SPY, I believe I did well for that one - even though more is possible.

In dissecting Bayer's results and future potential, let's start by stating the reason that Bayer is up so much in such a short time.

The reason is obviously centered around a potential resolution for the company's various legal issues regarding the Roundup scandal. The optimism started some months ago, but it's important to state that as yet, nothing is definitive. What has happened is that the U.S. Solicitor General has supported a review of the case by the U.S. Supreme Court, which certainly could limit future liability for the company's errors.

What else? Well, we have Bayer company turnaround and savings strategies. Over 13,000 people are being cut, with €2B in savings targeted by 2026. This is sort of positive because the turnout of such a savings program is usually that the revenue/earnings profile for a company is going to be different once it's over. Anyone who believes you can cut that number of staff without seeing financial or operational impacts is either working at a very poorly managed business or perhaps somewhat deluded.

There are other reasons the pharma business has done quite well, but this was something that I expected and included in my previous forecast as a sort of rebound and reversal reason, together with good performance in crop science, which has also come (and which I also expected). This is therefore not really a reason for me to increase my targets or expectations for the company.

Instead, the main reason I will be increasing my price target ("PT") and my thesis for Bayer is the political and legal changes we've been seeing, and because of the very nature of such changes (those being very volatile), that makes their eventual impact quite hard to forecast.

Q4 is not being reported until February - we have over a month until then at the time of writing this article. So Q3 '25 is the result we're looking at. The company, as of Q3 '25, is on track to deliver targets. Note that targets in this case include a Crop Science division that's barely growing and slow growth in both pharma and consumer health. It's important to know just how low the bar has been set for this company as of late, but also how low the valuation, with that in consideration, has been.

It should also be a "theme" for investors, the addition of "litigation-related provisions" at this point. The company's trajectory for both deleveraging and litigation is ongoing. The company is seeing growth in pharma for its legacy and new products and has seen good approvals for new products, also with good progress for new product and treatment areas, like cell therapy and gene therapy.

Recent FCF trends are good, despite a significant drop in Q3 '25 due to litigation payouts (a YoY drop of 48%), despite ongoing litigation payouts, and Bayer has been reducing its debt. The reduction in headcount is going according to plan.

It's important to mention that this recent upside for Bayer is incredibly new/recent - and given the current political, economic, and tariff climate, I wouldn't necessarily interpret the Solicitor General's decision as an outcome that's as favorable as the rate of recovery for Bayer has been saying.

Put another way, I believe the recent push we've been seeing is actually a significant overreaction to what amounts to a recommendation/filing.

My stance for Bayer, including the valuation thesis, is therefore centered around the likely impact and actual importance that I believe this event has. It's not insignificant - but it wouldn't, for instance, change the company's issues in pharma or the consumer sector. It also doesn't change the issues it has for some legacy products, such as generics for Xarelto or Eylea, and the weakness of its phase 3 pipeline. It removes, potentially at least, one risk but does not address everything.

Valuation for Bayer - Looking at a Gift Horse in the Mouth

You could characterize my thesis as this: looking a gift horse in the mouth. While I did expect an eventual and definitive positive outcome to the roundup legal issues, I did not expect it to come in the (implied) form that it's currently coming in. I don't believe the market as a whole did either, which is why we're up so much.

Put simply, I do not think Bayer has a noteworthy moat. Some of its businesses, when analyzed on their own, may have moats, but the group's ROIC remains so-so and remains, again, weighted down by M&A. Bayer is, without a doubt, the weakest of the big pharma firms I cover, and that's why I also put my fair value and estimates so low.

The current Bayer valuation is at about 8.6x P/E. This does not sound like much, but it was at half this amount about a year ago - and remember, the company is hardly growing at all, while also only paying a 0.26% current dividend yield.

I expected an upswing like this, but I did not expect it to be as significant or extreme as this.

But the company, at a longer-term P/E of 11-12x, closer to other pharma companies, Bayer has a significant and market-beating upside even from €41/share at about 18-20% annualized.

However, that estimate assumes that it manages to hold onto a significant overall upside of 60% in a short time, as well as expanding this upside even further in the medium term, an assumption that I believe could only materialize if we see significant upside from pharma, the consumer segment, or a complete resolution of the crop litigation.

I believe the likelihood of this is very, very low.

My previous target for Bayer was €35/share. That means that at the price of €41/share, Bayer has become a rotation target for me, unless I raise it. I raise the PT slightly to reflect the impact of the news, but I believe the lack of certainty and clarity with regards to an actual outcome means that anything above €40/share and a fair value of €45/share would be exuberant. Based on this, I essentially value the news, based on ~980 SO (Shares outstanding), at about €4.5-€5B, about 20-30% of its current litigation provision. This is already a high estimate, but I feel comfortable with it, given where the company has been going.

That means, however, that I believe Bayer is a Hold here. The company was a successful contrarian investment as per my last article, and my investment was under €29/share, and I have realized a market-beating rate of return by investing, but I believe the upside is mostly gone here. I believe more upside is characterized by premiumization that I view as unlikely given the struggles in pharma and consumer health.

I am therefore considering selling my stake here (but have not yet done so, as of the time of publishing).

Risks to Bayer

When it comes to risks, the most obvious two are the company's litigation in Roundup/Glyphosate in the U.S. and the pharma pricing risks on a forward basis, which could potentially prove to be margin-dilutive for the business on a forward basis. I still believe this to be the primary risk, despite the company's recent outperformance that we're able to see here.

Aside from this, I would also point out the company's history of poor capital allocation decisions. It requires discounting, and this remains part of the reason why I don't go much higher than €40/share, despite some analysts already pushing for €50-60 PTs. I believe that at this price, there are better options out there.

My updated €40/share PT for Bayer comes to $12/share for the BAYRY ADR.

Thesis

Bayer was a contrarian play with very good upside even in the most conservative and negative of scenarios - this upside has now been realized, as of January 2026, with over 60% RoR since my latest article. Despite a record of poor capital allocation and future litigation risks for some of the company's segments, I believed the current value for Bayer put it below all such concerns, where an upside was the only possible outcome. The success of such a thesis is always positive.

I now update my Bayer long-term fair value to €45, with a PT of €40/share.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which is not enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this, and a price target of €40/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me, I give the company a Hold rating as of January 2026.

This letter will highlight the results of the Thornburg Global Opportunities Fund for the twelve-month period ended 31 December 2025. It is published amidst the ongoing developments associated with the Russian war with Ukraine, an evolving inflation outlook in the U.S., Mideast tensions, and government policy uncertainty in the U.S. and elsewhere. The S&P 500 Index, the MSCI All Country World Index and the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index all reported positive total returns for calendar 2025, including Q4’2025. In brief, equities repriced higher in the face of uncertainty about the impact of U.S. trade policies. The U.S. dollar was broadly stable, and most bond markets rallied.

Your fund paid an ordinary income dividend of $0.61 per I share in December 2025 and a $2.45 long term capital gains dividend in Q4’2025, resulting in $3.06 in total dividends for calendar 2025. We are now in the 20th year of managing Thornburg Global Opportunities Fund. We believe that our strategy of owning a focused portfolio of firms with above average revenue growth that are value priced with respect to revenue/earnings/cash flow vis-à-vis the overall market has been a key ingredient to long term outperformance by Thornburg Global Opportunities Fund.

Listed in Table 1 in descending order are the 25 largest equity holdings in the fund as of 31 December 2025, along with their returns in calendar years 2025 and 2024. Returns are shown in $US, assuming reinvestment of dividends in shares of the firm. We also show the trailing 1-year and 5-year average annual revenue growth rates for these businesses through the latest reported period. For rough comparison:

  • The trailing 5-year weighted average revenue per share growth rate of the 2,517 firms included in the MSCI All Country World Index was +4.7%/year in $US terms through the end of the most recent reported trailing 12 month period. The average 1-year revenue per share growth rate of these same firms was +2.2%.
  • The trailing 5-year weighted average annual revenue per share growth rate of the 40 equity holdings in the Thornburg Global Opportunities portfolio was +11.6%/ year through the most recent reported trailing period, which ended 30 September 2025 for a majority of the firms listed. The weighted average 1 year revenue per share growth rate of these firms was +9.5% through the most recently reported 12-month period.
  • The weighted average portfolio revenue growth rates per share of your fund’s portfolio has significantly exceeded that of the MSCI All Country World Index.

Together, these firms comprised approximately 79% of the fund’s total assets as of 31 December 2025. Near- cash debt investments comprised 2% of fund assets, and 15 other equities comprised a total of approximately 19% of fund assets. Individual position sizes of the fund’s top 25 positions range from approximately 5.5% (Alphabet) to approximately 1.7% for those shown near the bottom of this list.

These are not trivial businesses. Weighted average revenue growth for the Thornburg Global Opportunities portfolio exceeded global GDP growth for the most recently reported trailing 1 year and 5-year periods. As previously described, the average revenue/share growth rates of these businesses significantly exceeded the revenue growth of the MSCI All Country World Index over the trailing 1 and 5-year periods. Most of these businesses emerged from the economic valley of the Covid period with their competitive positions intact or improved and ready to address the challenges and opportunities that lie ahead. A few of these are value priced “fixer-uppers” where we monitor the progress of structural improvement.

The reader will notice a high incidence of investments in firms tied to the digital economy, in producers of critical resources, and in financial intermediaries that we believe should benefit from interest rates determined primarily by free market forces. For now, there appears to be periodic fluctuation of investor confidence in the outlook for industrial commodity sector businesses.

Twenty-one equities made positive contributions to Thornburg Global Opportunities Fund portfolio performance of at least 0.10% during Q4’2025. Leading contributors to portfolio performance for the quarter included technology firms Samsung Electronics (SSNLF), Alphabet (GOOG), CACI International (CACI), and Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM); communications network operators Zegona Communications (ZEGLF), Orange (ORANY), and Converge Information & Communications; financials Citigroup (C), Bank of Ireland (BKRIF), BNP Paribas (BNPQF), NN Group (NNGPF), Capital One (COF), and Charles Schwab (SCHW); miners Freeport McMoran (FCX) and Mineral Resources (MALRF); consumer businesses Coca-Cola Icecek and TJX Companies (TJX); India conglomerate Reliance Industries; energy producer Total Energies (TTE); and pharmaceutical firm Eli Lilly (LLY).

Ten equities subtracted at least -0.10% from Thornburg Global Opportunities Fund portfolio performance during Q4’2025. The most significant detractors were e-commerce firms Alibaba Group (BABA), Tencent Holdings (TCEHY) and Meta Platforms (META); AT&T (T), consumer discretionary businesses Round One Corporation (RNDOF), Nien Made Enterprises and Galaxy Entertainment (GXYEF), industrial firms CATL and Techtronic Industries (TTNDY); and enterprise software developer SAP (SAP). We made various position size adjustments in recent months for portfolio diversification purposes, tax management, and to better balance the downside risk vs upside capital appreciation potential of individual positions.

Table 2 summarizes major sector weightings within the Global Opportunities Fund equity portfolio as of 31 December 2025 and selected prior quarter ends. The year-to-year reduction in Consumer Discretionary sector investments mostly reflects trims to position sizes of TJX Companies, Round One Corp, Mercedes Benz Group (MBGAF), Barratt Redrow (BTDPF), and Booking Holdings (BKNG). Increased investments in businesses in the Health Care sector, Information Technology sector, and Industrials sector were mostly funded by these trims.

Incoming economic data from around the world indicate a resilient global economy in the face of evolving U.S. trade policies, so far resisting falling into a recession. There are clear indications that global trade flows have been altered by new U.S. trade policies. Winners and losers among artificial intelligence linked businesses and multi-national producers of tradeable goods will become obvious in time, but the current outlook for many global businesses is uncertain. Prices of traded financial assets will be volatile with day-to-day news changing perceptions of near-term economic performance, interest rate policies, and political decisions We are paying attention to the abilities of your portfolio companies to manage cost inflation and maintain revenue and profit margins consistent with our expectations.

Our Investment Framework

  • Thornburg Global Opportunities Fund seeks capital appreciation from a focused portfolio of global equity investments. We believe the structure of the fund—built on our core investment principles of flexibility, focus, and value—provides a durable framework for value-added investing.
  • We urge shareholders of the fund to maintain a long-term investment perspective rather than placing too much emphasis on return figures that are available daily, weekly, monthly, and quarterly. A clear example of the need to keep a longer-term
  • investment perspective is illustrated by comparing the trailing 66-month return of Thornburg Global Opportunities fund as of 31 December 2025 [+212%] with the return from the single Covid onset quarter ending 31 March 2020 [-24.8%] that preceded this 66-month period. Most businesses in your portfolio have managed well through varying economic environments across business cycles. A few have potential to be much better businesses after being “fixed up.” We continue to follow our core investment principles of flexibility, focus, and value, as we have since your fund’s inception back in 2006.
  • Thank you for being a shareholder of Thornburg Global Opportunities Fund. Remember that you can monitor the holdings of Thornburg Global Opportunities Fund and other information respecting your fund on our website, www.thornburg.com.
